mnemonics.title: OOP Pillars
mnemonics.content: Remember OOP features with: 'P-E-A-A' = Polymorphism, Encapsulation, Abstraction, And Inheritance (or simply 'P-E-A-P': Please Eat All Pizzas for Polymorphism, Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance)

mnemonics.title: Access Specifiers Order
mnemonics.content: Public, Protected, Private: Think 'P-P-P' = Public (everyone), Protected (family only), Private (only me). Order: P-P-P = Please Protect Privacy.

mnemonics.title: Class vs Struct
mnemonics.content: Class = Private by default (C for Class = Confidential), Struct = Public by default (S for Struct = Social/Open).

acronyms.usage: A programming paradigm based on objects containing data and code
The entire system of OOP including classes, objects, and principles

acronyms.expansion: Single Responsibility, Open-Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion
acronyms.usage: Five design principles for writing maintainable OOP code
Three core concepts of OOP (some consider Abstraction as fourth)
A set of protocols and tools for building software applications, often using OOP principles

visualTips.visualization: Think of a class as an architect's blueprint for a house - it defines the structure but isn't the actual house itself. The object is the built house.
Visualize a medicine capsule - everything inside (data + functions) is wrapped in a protective shell, and you only see the external interface.
Draw inheritance as a family tree - parents pass traits (attributes/methods) to children (derived classes). The child gets all parent features plus their own.
Imagine different shapes (circle, square, triangle) all having a 'draw()' method. The same interface (draw) works differently for each shape.
Think of a TV remote - you press buttons (public interface) without knowing the complex electronics inside (hidden implementation).

analogies.analogy: Class is like a recipe card, Object is the actual cake made using that recipe. One recipe (class) can make many cakes (objects).
Inheritance is like genetic inheritance - children inherit eye color, hair color from parents. In programming, child classes inherit properties from parent classes.
Encapsulation is like a bank vault - money (data) is stored inside with restricted access. Only authorized methods (bank staff) can interact with it.
Polymorphism is like a person playing different roles - the same person (object) can be a teacher, father, and son (different behaviors in different contexts).
Abstraction is like driving a car - you use steering wheel, pedals (abstract interface) without understanding the internal combustion engine (implementation).
